summary: I grew up around Benham, Kentucky, where my father managed the Benham Credit Union. It was a coal camp town owned by U.S. Steel, and my family lived in company-owned houses there. Benham had its own company store and scrip system, separate from nearby Lynch. I remember the close-knit community and the way the company controlled much of daily life.

Lynch, Kentucky was where I spent much of my youth, living in the coal camps like Number Three and Number Five. The town was also owned by U.S. Steel, and we used scrip to buy things at the company store until the mid-1960s. I went to Lynch High School, where integration happened around 1964 or 1965, and sports helped bring people together. It was a tough but close community shaped by coal mining and company control.

U.S. Steel was the company that owned the coal camps and the houses in Benham and Lynch where I lived. They controlled everything, including the company store where we had to use scrip to buy our goods. My family’s life was deeply connected to U.S. Steel’s operations, as they were the main employer and landlord in the area. The company’s influence shaped much of our daily life and economy.

The Company Store was the only place we could buy everything from toys to clothes, and we had to use scrip that was only good in our town. In Lynch, the store was owned by U.S. Steel and was a central part of life for coal miners and their families. I remember how scrip limited us because you couldn’t use it in neighboring towns like Benham. Tennessee Ernie Ford even sang about the Company Store, which shows how well-known it was.

Tennessee Ernie Ford is famous for singing about the Company Store, which was a big part of our lives in Lynch and Benham. His song really captured what it was like to live in a coal camp where the company controlled everything. Hearing his music made me think about how deeply the company store affected us all growing up.

The Greater Mount Sinai Baptist Church was my church growing up in Lynch. My pastor, Ronnie Hampton, and I were neighbors and childhood friends. We grew up playing and sometimes fighting next door to each other, but the church was a big part of our community and upbringing. It was a place that helped shape our values and discipline.

Ronnie Hampton was my pastor and childhood friend; we lived next door to each other in Lynch’s coal camps. We grew up together, playing and sometimes fighting, but always close. He went to the same schools I did, and we shared many memories from our youth, including stories about discipline and growing up in the coal camps.

I attended Lynch High School during the time it was integrated around 1964 or 1965. The first couple of years were tough with fights, but sports helped bring everyone together. We had some of the best football and basketball teams, and playing together helped break down barriers between students. It was a pivotal time in my life and the community.

While I didn’t attend Benham High School, I knew people from there, including my pastor Ronnie Hampton who mentioned playing sports with my brother Bruce Hern who went to Benham. There was a strong rivalry between Benham and Lynch, especially in sports, which was a big part of life in the coal towns. The competition was fierce but also brought the communities together in some ways.

Cumberland, Kentucky was one of the neighboring towns to Lynch and Benham that we knew well. It was part of the regional rivalry in sports along with Harlan and Evarts. These towns were all coal mining communities with strong local identities and competitive spirit, especially in football and basketball.

Harlan County, Kentucky is the larger area that includes towns like Lynch, Benham, and Cumberland where I grew up. It was a coal mining region with a tough but close community. The county’s coal camps shaped much of my life and the lives of those around me during the mid-20th century.

Evarts, Kentucky was the main rival town to Lynch when it came to sports. Whenever Lynch played Evarts, there were often fights and a lot of tension. My parents wouldn’t even let me go to Evarts games because of the violence, like stone-throwing at buses. It was a fierce rivalry that really showed how divided the coal towns could be.

After graduating from high school, I received a scholarship to Southeast Community College and attended for one year. But I was young and eager to see the world, so I left college and went to New York instead. That year at Southeast Community College was a brief but important step before I moved on to new experiences.

I moved to New York after leaving college, living in Ossining near the penitentiary on 48 State Street. I worked at Fisher Body Chevrolet in nearby Tarrytown for about six months before being drafted into the Army. It was a big change from Lynch, and I was scared walking near the prison, but it was my chance to see the world beyond Kentucky.

Fisher Body was the place I worked in New York, specifically at the Fisher Body Chevrolet plant in Tarrytown. It was my first job outside of Kentucky after leaving college. Working there was part of my brief time in New York before I was drafted into the Army during the Vietnam War era.

I was drafted during the Vietnam War after spending six months in New York. I didn’t want to go to Vietnam, so I signed up for an extra year in the Army to avoid being sent there initially, but eventually I served a full year in Vietnam. It was the longest and hardest year of my life, and seeing children forced to fight was especially painful. Coming back home was difficult, and I think that war was one we never really won.

I served in the United States Army during the Vietnam War. After basic training, I was sent to Vietnam for a year, which was the toughest time of my life. I signed up for an extra year to avoid going to Vietnam initially, but eventually I served there. The Army experience changed me and made it hard to adjust when I returned home.

After my military service, I went to work in the coal mines in Lynch, Kentucky. Coal mining was the main job that paid well in our town, especially in the 1970s. It was hard work but came with good wages and benefits, and it was the backbone of our community’s economy. Many people in Lynch depended on coal mining for their livelihood.

Ed Miracle was the coach at Lynch High School who had a strict rule that you couldn’t play basketball unless you played football first. I remember that well because I could have played basketball but didn’t want to play football. He was a tough coach who ran the sports programs and made sure we followed his rules. Sports were a big deal in our community, and he was a central figure in that.
